questions.question: In the four-station wireless geometry A–B–C–D (where A is in range of B, B is in range of C, and A is NOT in range of C), what is the name for the problem that occurs when both A and C try to send to B simultaneously?
options: Exposed terminal problem
Hidden terminal problem
Collision domain overlap
Carrier-sense failure
questions.explanation: A and C are hidden from each other (out of range) but both transmit to the same receiver B, causing a collision at B that neither sender can detect. This is the hidden terminal problem.

questions.question: Why can wireless stations NOT use CSMA/CD (collision detection) the way wired Ethernet does?
options: Wireless channels are full-duplex so collisions never occur
A transmitter's own signal is millions of times stronger than any received signal, making simultaneous listen impossible
Wireless frames are too short for the collision window
The MAC address is not available at the physical layer in 802.11
questions.explanation: A transmitting station's antenna output is roughly +20 dBm while a received signal at 100 m is about -50 dBm — a ~70 dB (10 million times) ratio. The receiver's AGC cannot detect another signal under that flood, so collision detection during transmission is hardware-impossible.

questions.question: In MACA, a station that overhears a CTS frame must defer for how long?
options: Until the RTS transmission completes
For one slot time, then retry
For the entire data frame whose length is announced in the CTS
Until it hears the sender go silent
questions.explanation: The CTS echoes the data-frame length. Any station in range of the receiver that overhears the CTS defers for the full duration of that data frame, preventing hidden-terminal collisions at the receiver.

questions.question: Station C is in range of sender B but NOT in range of receiver A. B is currently transmitting to A. Which statement best describes C in the MACA model?
options: C is a hidden terminal and must defer for the full data frame
C is an exposed terminal: it hears B's RTS but its own transmission to D would not collide at A or D, so it may transmit after the CTS window
C must wait for B's ACK before it can transmit
C will overhear the CTS and defer for the data frame
questions.explanation: C hears B's RTS and defers briefly (until the CTS window closes), but C does NOT hear the CTS because it is out of range of A. Since C's transmission to D would not reach A (the receiver), the deferral is unnecessary — C is an exposed terminal that MACA allows to transmit during the data phase.

questions.question: What three additions did MACAW (Bharghavan 1994) make to the original MACA protocol?
options: Per-frame ACK, OFDMA channel bonding, and NAV timer
Per-frame ACK, RRDT flow-control frame, and a per-link retry counter visible to both ends
Exponential backoff, CSMA/CA preamble, and the Duration field
Per-frame ACK, WPA2 encryption, and MIMO spatial streams
questions.explanation: MACAW added (1) a per-frame ACK so the sender discovers a collision at the receiver, (2) an RRDT (Request-Ready-To-Send) for receiver-driven flow control, and (3) a per-link retry counter shared between the two endpoints. 802.11 added the NAV, CSMA/CA, and exponential backoff on top.

questions.question: In the textbook's MACA timing example (30-byte RTS/CTS at 1 Mbps, 5 µs propagation, 20 µs CTS turnaround), when is station C (in range of sender A, not in range of receiver B) free to transmit again after the RTS?
options: After 240 µs (when the RTS finishes)
After 265 µs (RTS + propagation to B)
After 505 µs (when the CTS transmission at B would have finished)
After 8535 µs (when the full data frame plus propagation completes)
questions.explanation: C hears the RTS end at 240 µs and defers until the CTS window closes. The CTS starts at B after 240+5+20=265 µs and takes 240 µs to transmit, finishing at 505 µs. C is free at 505 µs. D (in range of B only) must defer until after the data frame ends at ~8535 µs.
